answer:
**Fichte's *Denkfreiheit* (freedom of thought)** is an inalienable human right essential to personhood, morality, and humanity's progress, which no prince or state may legitimately suppress.[^1] It encompasses not only the internal capacity to think freely—resisting blind association of ideas through self-directed reason—but also the right to communicate one's thoughts and to receive others' freely, without coercion or censorship.[^2] This right derives from the moral law imprinted by God within conscience, which demands absolute obedience without external compulsion; alienating it would reduce humans to animals.[^3] Princes derive authority from a social contract transferring only *alienable* rights to external actions (not inner dispositions or thought); they must protect rights, not infringe them, even under pretext of preventing "error" or promoting happiness.[^4] Suppressing *Denkfreiheit* risks violent revolution, as it blocks gradual enlightenment toward better constitutions.[^5]

**Charles Comte's *liberté***, by contrast, is the state of a person facing *no obstacles* from others to the natural development of their being or innocent exercise of faculties—a negation of *slavery*, where humans treat others as property, seize labor's fruits, or direct their lives.[^6] It requires recognizing no one as another's property, protecting labor's products, self-directed occupations, free movement/association/education, accountability of authorities, and judgment of actions by universal principles (not masters' caprice).[^7] Liberty emerges when slavery's *principles* (possession of persons) and *means* (coercion, isolation, disarmament) vanish; partial removal yields "liberties," full abolition yields true *liberty*.[^8] Comte notes Dunoyer's differing sense but aligns liberty with anti-slavery, tying it to property as labor's fruit.[^9]

**Charles Dunoyer's *liberté*** is *power* to use faculties easily, acquired by freeing them from *internal obstacles* (ignorance, vices, passions) via *industry* (skill, development) and *morality* (self-restraint, non-harm to others, resistance to injustice).[^10] It grows with civilization: more knowledge/strength enlarges action's sphere; temperance preserves forces; justice/reciprocity ensures security.[^11] Not innate or absolute (bounded by nature), it is *relative*—measured by progress in all arts/habits, where violence/domination destroys it, while peaceful industry/morals extend it.[^12] Liberty crowns industrial society, where activity targets production/exchange, not rule.[^13]

### Key Differences
Fichte's *Denkfreiheit* is narrow/intellectual: an absolute, God-given *right* to uncensored thought/communication, primarily against *state censorship* (princes as rights-protectors, not truth-arbiters).[^14] Comte/Dunoyer's *liberty* is broad/practical: *capacity/power* from self/societal improvement, overcoming *all obstacles* (personal vices, slavery, domination)—more economic/social, rooted in property/labor/non-aggression, where thought-freedom fits as one faculty among many enabled by industry/morals.[^15] Fichte defends against *external suppression* by rulers; Comte/Dunoyer emphasize *internal development* against ignorance/slavery, viewing liberty as *result* of progress (not innate right).[^16] Fichte risks revolution if thought is chained; Comte/Dunoyer see true liberty in civilized, non-exploitative society.[^17]

[^1]: Fichte, *Zurückforderung der Denkfreiheit...*, ch.1, [https://falsafa.corpus/works/johann-gottlieb-fichte-zuruckforderung-der-denkfreiheit-bookde/01/translation/#p-effd89](https://falsafa.corpus/works/johann-gottlieb-fichte-zuruckforderung-der-denkfreiheit-bookde/01/translation/#p-effd89).

[^2]: Ibid., ch.2, [https://falsafa.corpus/works/johann-gottlieb-fichte-zuruckforderung-der-denkfreiheit-bookde/02/translation/#p-e62dae](https://falsafa.corpus/works/johann-gottlieb-fichte-zuruckforderung-der-denkfreiheit-bookde/02/translation/#p-e62dae).

[^3]: Ibid., [https://falsafa.corpus/works/johann-gottlieb-fichte-zuruckforderung-der-denkfreiheit-bookde/02/translation/#p-5ec8d2](https://falsafa.corpus/works/johann-gottlieb-fichte-zuruckforderung-der-denkfreiheit-bookde/02/translation/#p-5ec8d2).

[^4]: Ibid., [https://falsafa.corpus/works/johann-gottlieb-fichte-zuruckforderung-der-denkfreiheit-bookde/02/translation/#p-be3fae](https://falsafa.corpus/works/johann-gottlieb-fichte-zuruckforderung-der-denkfreiheit-bookde/02/translation/#p-be3fae).

[^5]: Ibid., ch.1, [https://falsafa.corpus/works/johann-gottlieb-fichte-zuruckforderung-der-denkfreiheit-bookde/01/translation/#p-47d2fc](https://falsafa.corpus/works/johann-gottlieb-fichte-zuruckforderung-der-denkfreiheit-bookde/01/translation/#p-47d2fc).

[^6]: Comte, *Traité de la propriété: VOL I*, ch.3, [https://falsafa.corpus/works/charles-comte-traite-de-la-propriete-vol-i-2c7a99/03-de-ce-qui-constitue-la-liberte/translation/](https://falsafa.corpus/works/charles-comte-traite-de-la-propriete-vol-i-2c7a99/03-de-ce-qui-constitue-la-liberte/translation/).

[^7]: Ibid., [https://falsafa.corpus/works/charles-comte-traite-de-la-propriete-vol-i-2c7a99/03-de-ce-qui-constitue-la-liberte/translation/#p-86c544](https://falsafa.corpus/works/charles-comte-traite-de-la-propriete-vol-i-2c7a99/03-de-ce-qui-constitue-la-liberte/translation/#p-86c544).

[^8]: Ibid., [https://falsafa.corpus/works/charles-comte-traite-de-la-propriete-vol-i-2c7a99/03-de-ce-qui-constitue-la-liberte/translation/#p-025e2a](https://falsafa.corpus/works/charles-comte-traite-de-la-propriete-vol-i-2c7a99/03-de-ce-qui-constitue-la-liberte/translation/#p-025e2a).

[^9]: Ibid., [https://falsafa.corpus/works/charles-comte-traite-de-la-propriete-vol-i-2c7a99/03-de-ce-qui-constitue-la-liberte/translation/#p-2ffb13](https://falsafa.corpus/works/charles-comte-traite-de-la-propriete-vol-i-2c7a99/03-de-ce-qui-constitue-la-liberte/translation/#p-2ffb13).

[^11]: Ibid., intro., [https://falsafa.corpus/works/charles-dunoyer-nouveau-traite-deconomie-vol-i-6da8ce/00-introduction/translation/#p-67acca](https://falsafa.corpus/works/charles-dunoyer-nouveau-traite-deconomie-vol-i-6da8ce/00-introduction/translation/#p-67acca); ch.1, [https://falsafa.corpus/works/charles-dunoyer-nouveau-traite-deconomie-vol-i-6da8ce/01-ce-que-lauteur-entend-par-le-mot-liberte/translation/#p-583c6c](https://falsafa.corpus/works/charles-dunoyer-nouveau-traite-deconomie-vol-i-6da8ce/01-ce-que-lauteur-entend-par-le-mot-liberte/translation/#p-583c6c).

[^12]: Ibid., ch.1, [https://falsafa.corpus/works/charles-dunoyer-nouveau-traite-deconomie-vol-i-6da8ce/01-ce-que-lauteur-entend-par-le-mot-liberte/translation/#p-d87146](https://falsafa.corpus/works/charles-dunoyer-nouveau-traite-deconomie-vol-i-6da8ce/01-ce-que-lauteur-entend-par-le-mot-liberte/translation/#p-d87146).

[^13]: Ibid., intro., [https://falsafa.corpus/works/charles-dunoyer-nouveau-traite-deconomie-vol-i-6da8ce/00-introduction/translation/#p-ecca74](https://falsafa.corpus/works/charles-dunoyer-nouveau-traite-deconomie-vol-i-6da8ce/00-introduction/translation/#p-ecca74).

[^14]: Fichte, ch.2, [https://falsafa.corpus/works/johann-gottlieb-fichte-zuruckforderung-der-denkfreiheit-bookde/02/translation/#p-44e1de](https://falsafa.corpus/works/johann-gottlieb-fichte-zuruckforderung-der-denkfreiheit-bookde/02/translation/#p-44e1de).

[^15]: Comte, ch.3, [https://falsafa.corpus/works/charles-comte-traite-de-la-propriete-vol-i-2c7a99/03-de-ce-qui-constitue-la-liberte/translation/#p-1c5676](https://falsafa.corpus/works/charles-comte-traite-de-la-propriete-vol-i-2c7a99/03-de-ce-qui-constitue-la-liberte/translation/#p-1c5676); Dunoyer, ch.1, [https://falsafa.corpus/works/charles-dunoyer-nouveau-traite-deconomie-vol-i-6da8ce/01-ce-que-lauteur-entend-par-le-mot-liberte/translation/#p-e80d8f](https://falsafa.corpus/works/charles-dunoyer-nouveau-traite-deconomie-vol-i-6da8ce/01-ce-que-lauteur-entend-par-le-mot-liberte/translation/#p-e80d8f).
